subject: Science
chapter: The World of Metals and Non-metals

key_points: Chemical reactivity varies significantly among metals; some react vigorously with water, others only with acids.
Metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series can displace hydrogen from dilute acids.
Metal oxides are basic because they form hydroxides in water, whereas non-metal oxides are acidic.
Displacement reactions occur when a more reactive metal pushes out a less reactive metal from its salt solution.
Corrosion is a slow process where metals are eaten away by the action of air, moisture, or chemicals.
examples: Vigorous Reaction: Sodium (Na) reacting with water (stored in kerosene to prevent fire).
Displacement: Iron nail in Copper Sulphate solution (Blue solution turns Green).
Acidic Oxide: Sulphur Dioxide ($SO_2$) dissolved in water forms Sulphurous acid ($H_2SO_3$).
Non-reactive Metals: Gold (Au) and Platinum (Pt) do not react with water or dilute acids.
Galvanization: Coating iron with Zinc (Zn) to prevent rusting.
formulas: Metal + Water → Metal Hydroxide + Hydrogen gas ($H_2$)
Metal + Dilute Acid → Metal Salt + Hydrogen gas ($H_2$)
Displacement: $Fe + CuSO_4 ightarrow FeSO_4 + Cu$ (Iron displaces Copper)
exam_tips: The 'Pop' sound test is the standard way to identify the presence of Hydrogen gas.
Copper does not react with dilute Hydrochloric acid ($HCl$) even on heating.
Remember the color change in displacement: Blue $CuSO_4$ becomes Green $FeSO_4$ when iron is added.
Phosphorus is a very reactive non-metal and is stored in water to prevent it from catching fire in air.
